agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed reduction of delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days in a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets, serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a storage cost optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is dangerously incorrect.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete dataset capturing a delivery transaction: sender, recipient, timestamps, proof of delivery, item description, customs declarations, and associated financial data." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, GPS coordinates. Used in dispute resolution and insurance claims." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The rules governing how long delivery records are stored before deletion. Driven by regulatory, contractual, and operational requirements — not storage costs." },
      { "term": "Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by B2B customers to satisfy their own legal and regulatory obligations (tax filings, audit trails, customs declarations)." },
      { "term": "Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)", "definition": "A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, which does NOT apply when retention is required for legal compliance obligations." },
      { "term": "Statutory Retention Period", "definition": "The minimum period a business is legally required to retain records, as mandated by tax, customs, or commercial law in the operating jurisdiction." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records containing VAT-relevant transaction data must be retained for the statutory minimum period required by each EU member state's tax authority (typically 5-10 years; e.g., Germany 10 years, France 6 years, Netherlands 7 years).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Statutory tax law. Violation exposes the company to fines, back-taxes, and criminal liability. No VP or policy update can override national tax law.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record containing VAT transaction data is deleted before the statutory retention period of its applicable jurisdiction has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "UK delivery records must be retained for a minimum of 6 years to satisfy HMRC requirements for VAT and business transaction records.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "HMRC VAT Notice 700/21 and Companies Act 2006 require retention of transaction records. Non-compliance results in penalties and inability to defend tax audits.",
      "testable_assertion": "No UK-jurisdiction delivery record is deleted before 6 years from the transaction date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Cross-border delivery records involving customs declarations must be retained for the period required by the Union Customs Code (minimum 3 years, but member states may extend to 10 years).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "EU customs law (Regulation 952/2013). Customs authorities can audit retrospectively and demand documentation. Deletion destroys legally required evidence.",
      "testable_assertion": "No cross-border delivery record with customs data is deleted before the applicable customs retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "B2B customer delivery data must be retained for the contractual period agreed with each B2B customer, which must be at least as long as the statutory minimum for their compliance obligations.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Contractual obligation, not statutory law. However, breach of contract exposes the platform to liability and customer churn. The specific period is policy-adjustable per contract.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record used by a B2B customer for compliance reporting is deleted before the contractual retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Proof of Delivery records must be retained long enough to cover the limitation period for consumer disputes, chargebacks, and insurance claims (typically 6 years in UK under Limitation Act 1980, varies by EU member state).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If POD data is deleted and a consumer or insurer raises a claim within the limitation period, the platform cannot defend itself. This is an existential litigation risk.",
      "testable_assertion": "No Proof of Delivery record is deleted before the applicable civil limitation period for the delivery's jurisdiction has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "GDPR does not require deletion of delivery records within 90 days. Article 17(3)(b) explicitly exempts data retained for compliance with a legal obligation under EU or member state law.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Misapplying GDPR as a justification for premature deletion actually creates MORE legal risk than retaining data with proper legal basis under Art. 6(1)(c).",
      "testable_assertion": "Data retention policy documentation cites the specific legal basis (Art. 6(1)(c)) for retention beyond the minimum necessary for the original processing purpose."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A German tax authority audits transactions from 4 years ago, requesting delivery documentation as VAT evidence.", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records from that period must be available and complete. Under the proposed 90-day policy, this data would have been destroyed 3.75 years earlier, resulting in inability to comply with the audit." },
    { "scenario": "A B2B logistics customer is audited by their own regulator and requests delivery data from 2 years ago to satisfy their compliance obligations.", "expected_behavior": "Platform must provide the requested data. Under the 90-day policy, the data would be gone, breaching the B2B contractual obligation and potentially exposing the customer to regulatory penalties." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer disputes a delivery from 11 months ago, claiming non-receipt, and initiates a chargeback.", "expected_behavior": "Platform must produce Proof of Delivery to contest the chargeback. Under the 90-day policy, the POD is already deleted, and the platform loses the dispute by default." },
    { "scenario": "An insurance claim for a high-value shipment lost 18 months ago requires delivery chain documentation.", "expected_behavior": "Full delivery chain records must be available to support or defend the claim. A 90-day policy would have destroyed this evidence." },
    { "scenario": "A cross-border customs audit requests documentation for a shipment that passed through EU customs 2 years ago.", "expected_behavior": "Customs declarations and associated delivery records must be producible. Failure to comply results in customs penalties." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Auto-deleting at 90 days exposes the company to: (1) Tax authority penalties across multiple EU jurisdictions and UK — potentially millions in fines and back-taxes; (2) Undefendable chargebacks and insurance claims — direct revenue loss; (3) B2B contract breach liability — damages claims from enterprise customers who lose their own compliance capability; (4) Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory penalty or lost enterprise contract.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL. The platform would be unable to: respond to tax audits, fulfill B2B data requests, defend delivery disputes, or support customs inquiries. Operations and legal teams would be flying blind for any event older than 90 days.",
    "user": "B2C consumers lose the ability to raise legitimate delivery disputes beyond 90 days. B2B customers lose access to data they contractually depend on for their own regulatory compliance — this is a service-breaking change for enterprise accounts, not an optimization.",
    "risk": "EXTREME. (1) Regulatory non-compliance in multiple jurisdictions simultaneously; (2) Breach of contract with B2B customers; (3) Loss of legal defensibility in disputes and litigation; (4) Potential director-level personal liability for knowingly destroying legally required records. This is not a technical risk — it is a legal and existential business risk.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Cannot fulfill their own compliance obligations. May face regulatory penalties. Likely to churn and pursue breach-of-contract claims." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Lose ability to dispute deliveries, request proof of delivery, or escalate complaints beyond 90 days." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax Team", "impact": "Cannot respond to VAT audits. Company exposed to fines, back-taxes, and potential criminal proceedings in jurisdictions with strict record-keeping laws (e.g., Germany)." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance Team", "impact": "Cannot defend against claims, produce evidence for litigation, or demonstrate regulatory compliance. Liability exposure across all operating jurisdictions." },
      { "group": "Insurance Partners", "impact": "Claims processing becomes impossible for any shipment older than 90 days. May affect insurance terms and premiums." },
      { "group": "Executive Leadership", "impact": "Personal liability risk in jurisdictions where directors are responsible for ensuring lawful record retention (e.g., German GmbH Geschäftsführer liability)." },
      { "group": "Engineering Team", "impact": "Framing this as 'no business logic changes' creates organizational risk — if implemented without legal review, engineers could be complicit in regulatory non-compliance." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 40,
    "reasoning": "Confidence is low because the proposal as stated contains a fundamental contradiction: it claims 'no business logic changes' while proposing a change that directly violates multiple statutory retention requirements in the platform's operating jurisdictions. The exact retention periods vary by EU member state and require legal counsel to confirm jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction. Without knowing the specific countries served, exact B2B contract terms, and types of goods shipped (e.g., regulated goods with additional documentation requirements), the precise minimum retention periods cannot be fully specified.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "90-day retention violates UK HMRC requirements (6 years)",
      "90-day retention violates German tax record requirements (10 years)",
      "B2B customers will be materially harmed",
      "GDPR does not mandate 90-day deletion of delivery records",
      "This is NOT merely an infrastructure optimization"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact statutory retention periods for each specific EU member state the platform operates in",
      "Specific contractual retention obligations in existing B2B agreements",
      "Whether any tiered retention approach (hot/warm/cold storage) could address storage costs while maintaining compliance",
      "Whether current 7-year policy was originally chosen to satisfy a specific jurisdiction or was a conservative catch-all"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Platform processes deliveries involving VAT-liable transactions",
      "B2B contracts include data availability commitments (standard in logistics SaaS)",
      "Cross-border deliveries involve customs documentation",
      "The 7-year existing policy was chosen to broadly comply with EU and UK statutory requirements"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The engineering team claims this is 'no business logic changes' — an infrastructure optimization only. This is factually incorrect. Data retention for delivery records in EU/UK logistics is a legal and contractual obligation, not a storage preference. Reducing retention from 7 years to 90 days would violate tax law (HMRC, German AO, French CGI), customs regulations (Union Customs Code), B2B contractual obligations, and eliminate the platform's ability to defend against disputes and claims. This change CANNOT proceed as framed.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["B2B Customers", "B2C Consumers", "Finance/Tax", "Legal/Compliance", "Executive Leadership", "Insurance Partners"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day deletion proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered storage (hot storage for 90 days, cold/archive storage for the full statutory period) to reduce costs while maintaining compliance",
        "C: Engage legal counsel for a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction analysis to determine the true minimum retention period per record type, then implement differentiated retention policies"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C first (legal review), then Option B (tiered storage) for cost optimization. The storage cost problem is real and valid — but the solution is cheaper storage tiers, not illegal deletion. UNDER NO CIRCUMSTANCES should Option A's 90-day deletion proceed without explicit sign-off from legal counsel in every operating jurisdiction."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Need to know: (1) Which specific EU member states does the platform operate in? (2) What are the exact retention terms in existing B2B contracts? (3) Does the platform handle regulated goods (pharmaceuticals, food, hazardous materials) with additional documentation retention requirements? (4) What is the current storage cost, and what savings does the 90-day proposal target?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering Team", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance"],
      "options": [
        "Gather jurisdiction list and B2B contract terms before any further analysis",
        "Proceed with conservative 7-year retention while legal review is conducted"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Gather the missing context. The storage cost savings should be quantified against the regulatory penalty exposure to demonstrate why tiered storage is the correct approach."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting delivery records at 90 days violates statutory tax record retention requirements in the UK (6 years) and EU member states (5-10 years depending on jurisdiction).", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1, BR-2" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting customs-related delivery records at 90 days violates EU Union Customs Code retention requirements.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting B2B customer delivery data at 90 days likely breaches contractual data availability commitments.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting Proof of Delivery at 90 days eliminates the platform's ability to defend disputes and claims within the civil limitation period.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "Framing this as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect and dangerous — it bypasses the legal and compliance review that a data retention change in regulated EU/UK markets requires.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1 through BR-6" }
  ]
}
